Workshop 1: ggplot2 and dplyr

Introduction

In this workshop we get to grips with the basics of using ggplot2 to produce plots, and dplyr for manipulating data.

For some of you, this workshop will be largely revision: Nonetheless we strongly recommend you run through it to refamiliarise yourself with the basics of the tidyverse set of packages.

For others, this may be new - you may be familiar with R and RStudio (and even RMarkdown), but may not be familiar with the tidyverse set of packages: They take some getting used to, but contain a consistent, powerful set of functions for wrangling data and producing plots. Nonetheless, they’re not compulsory - we don’t mind if you prefer base R functions, or data.table, or anything else for that matter - as long as you understand what you’re doing and can effectively produce graphics or manipulate data then all is good.

Before you start, make sure you can Knit this document in RStudio.

We start by loading in the tidyverse and palmerpenguins packages (for data).

library(tidyverse)
## ── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
## ✔ dplyr     1.2.0     ✔ readr     2.1.5
## ✔ forcats   1.0.0     ✔ stringr   1.5.2
## ✔ ggplot2   4.0.0     ✔ tibble    3.3.0
## ✔ lubridate 1.9.4     ✔ tidyr     1.3.2
## ✔ purrr     1.2.1     
## ── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
## ✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
## ✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
## ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors
library(palmerpenguins)
## 
## Attaching package: 'palmerpenguins'
## 
## The following objects are masked from 'package:datasets':
## 
##     penguins, penguins_raw
pebguins <- penguins

Some notes:

  • When you run this code chunk you’ll get some startup messages that some packages are being attached, and that some functions conflict with others (dplyr::lag() for example will now be overriding stats::lag()). These are fine, as this is what we want, but it is important to take note of these, as sometimes loading a package may override a function that you want kept1.

  • The palmerpenguins package includes the penguins data which we’ll be using below. When you loaded this R Markdown document into RStudio you may have been prompted (via a yellow bar at the top) to install these packages. If you haven’t, and get an error - that’s OK, just click on the Packages menu in the bottom right and “Install”, then type in the name of the package.

  • Naming the code chunk setup means that it is treated specially by R markdown - when you run any of the chunks further down, it will ensure that the setup chunk is run first. This can be quite handy when writing R markdown documents.

Charting with ggplot2

Recall that the ggplot2 package uses the grammar of graphics to create charts. It’s a little bit ‘wordy’ - i.e. you tend to have to write a reasonable amount of code to get a chart, but it is very consistent, and once past very simple charts is very powerful. Just about everything about the chart can be tweaked.

We start with a simple scatter plot of the penguins data:

ggplot(data = pebguins) +
  geom_point(
    mapping = aes(x = flipper_length_mm,
                  y = body_mass_g
    ))
## Warning: Removed 2 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
## (`geom_point()`).

Recall that the recipe for a ggplot chart is:

  1. Use ggplot to create an empty chart.
  2. Assign some data to it via the data argument to ggplot. We’ve used the penguins data.
  3. Choose a geometry layer, in this case geom_point for points.
  4. Map the aesthetics (a mapping from features of the chart to columns in our data). In this case we’ve mapped the x axis to flipper_length_mm and the y axis to body_mass_g.

Notice that we’ve named all the parameters (data, mapping, x, y) that we’re passing to the functions (ggplot, geom_point, aes). This is just style - we could have just as easily used ggplot(penguins) + geom_point(aes(flipper_length_mm,body_mass_g)) as in each case the named parameter is just the first (or in the case of y the second) argument to each of the functions. We recommend you name parameters rather than not: While it takes more typing (you can use tab-complete, i.e. type first few characters, press TAB, to speed it up) it allows your code to be self-documenting. While I always remember that the first argument to ggplot() is data and the first to geom_point() is mapping, I don’t always remember that the second argument to aes is y - and I have no idea what the 3rd argument is!

Try yourself

  1. Try adding colour = 'purple' as an additional argument to geom_point, after the mapping argument.
  2. Try adding colour = 'purple' as an additional argument to aes inside geom_point. Which is the appropriate spot for colours points purple? This is setting an aesthetic.
  3. Try mapping the colour aesthetic to the species column of the penguins data.
#1. 
ggplot(data = pebguins) +
  geom_point(
    mapping = aes(x = flipper_length_mm,
                  y = body_mass_g), colour = 'purple')
## Warning: Removed 2 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
## (`geom_point()`).

#2.
ggplot(data = penguins) +
  geom_point(
    mapping = aes(x = flipper_length_mm,
                  y = body_mass_g, colour = 'purple'))
## Warning: Removed 2 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
## (`geom_point()`).

#3
ggplot(data = penguins) +
  geom_point(
    mapping = aes(x = flipper_length_mm,
                  y = body_mass_g, colour = species))
## Warning: Removed 2 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
## (`geom_point()`).

You should have noticed that the values in the flipper_length_mm and body_mass_g are rounded, so that the points appear to be uniformly spaced in some instances. Indeed, it is feasible that there are pairs of points in the data that are identical, so that the points might be placed directly on top of each other. We can’t tell in this plot if this is the case or not, as there is no depth to the chart.

There is two potential ways to fix this:

  1. Adding some jitter via using geom_jitter. This adds some random noise to the x or y values before plotting so we can tell if there is multiple points close to each other.

  2. Add some transparency to the points by setting alpha (the aesthetic for transparency) to something smaller than 1 (alpha = 1 is the default, which is opaque. alpha = 0 is fully transparent).

Both of these methods will allow the ink density to reveal where there is more data - areas with more points will appear darker than areas with fewer points.

Try yourself

#1
 ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = flipper_length_mm,
                  y = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_point(aes(colour = species, alpha = 0.5))
## Warning: Removed 2 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
## (`geom_point()`).

#2
 ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = flipper_length_mm,
                  y = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_point(aes(colour = species))+
geom_jitter(colour = 'blue',alpha = 0.3, width = 0.5, height = 0.5)
## Warning: Removed 2 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
## (`geom_point()`).
## Removed 2 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
## (`geom_point()`).

  1. Implement each of the above methods to assess whether they help in this case.
  2. You could try both at once, or try altering the amount of jitter (see the help for geom_jitter, or Google!)

In the example above we used just one layer, geom_point, but we can add multiple layers if we like. e.g.

ggplot(data = penguins) +
  geom_point(mapping = aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g, colour = species)) +
  geom_smooth(mapping = aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g))
## `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'
## Warning: Removed 2 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
## (`stat_smooth()`).
## Warning: Removed 2 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
## (`geom_point()`).

Note the repetition here in that the mapping is defined twice. We can simplify this by noting that the ggplot function also has a mapping argument, and any layer without one will inherit this mapping, simplifying the code to:

ggplot(data = penguins, mapping = aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth()
## `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'
## Warning: Removed 2 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
## (`stat_smooth()`).
## Warning: Removed 2 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
## (`geom_point()`).

Ofcourse, we can override the mapping if we like, e.g. to map colour to species.

Try yourself

  1. Add a mapping argument to geom_point() in the code above to map the colour of points to species.
  2. Alternatively, modify the aes() function in the ggplot bit to map the colour of points and the smooth to species.
#1
ggplot(data = penguins, mapping = aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_point(mapping = aes(colour = species)) +
  geom_smooth()
## `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'
## Warning: Removed 2 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
## (`stat_smooth()`).
## Warning: Removed 2 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
## (`geom_point()`).

#2
ggplot(data = penguins, mapping = aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g, colour = species)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth()
## `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'
## Warning: Removed 2 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range (`stat_smooth()`).
## Removed 2 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
## (`geom_point()`).

Recall that for one-dimensional summaries we can use histograms or density plots, e.g.

ggplot(data = penguins, mapping = aes(x = flipper_length_mm)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(fill = species))
## `stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.
## Warning: Removed 2 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
## (`stat_bin()`).

ggplot(data = penguins, mapping = aes(x = flipper_length_mm)) +
  geom_histogram()
## `stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.
## Warning: Removed 2 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
## (`stat_bin()`).

Obviously the histogram doesn’t require the y aesthetic to be mapped, as it is computed by the histogram.

Try yourself

  1. Modify the above to use geom_density() instead.
  2. Try mapping colour or fill to species of the density and histogram. You should notice that the histogram stacks (i.e. the second histogram bars start on top of the first), while the densities start at zero.
  3. Try altering the histogram coloured by species so the bars don’t stack. Check out the position argument to geom_histogram for this (Google, or look up help).
  4. Have a play with the adjust argument of geom_density: Values greater than 1 will result in more smoothing, values less than one less smoothing2.
  5. Switch to using geom_boxplot() instead. Note that with boxplots you have the option of mapping the y aesthetic to something such as species - ofcourse you can still map the colour or fill as well!
ggplot(data = penguins, mapping = aes(x = flipper_length_mm)) +
  geom_histogram()
## `stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.
## Warning: Removed 2 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
## (`stat_bin()`).

ggplot(data = penguins, mapping = aes(x = flipper_length_mm)) +
  geom_density()
## Warning: Removed 2 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
## (`stat_density()`).

Recall that you can label charts using the labs command. Remember that the arguments to labs are the aesthetics (i.e. x, y, colour, fill) in addition to title, subtitle, caption.

ggplot(data = penguins, mapping = aes(x = flipper_length_mm)) +
  labs(title = "Flipper lengths of Penguins (mm)", 
       x = "Flipper length (mm)",
       subtitle = "Source: https://allisonhorst.github.io/palmerpenguins/",
       caption = "Data from the `Palmer Penguins` dataset") +
  geom_histogram(fill= "lightblue", bins = 30)

Try yourself

  1. Modify some of your above plots to provide better axis labels, a title and perhaps a caption with the data source or with details of who made the plot (you!)

Data manipulation with dplyr

Recall that dplyr is the main package in the tidyverse for manipulating data. There are essentially 6 key verbs/functions for you to know about in addition to a bunch of handy shortcuts:

  • filter: choose rows based on some criteria.
  • arrange: reorder rows based on some criteria.
  • select: choose or reorder columns.
  • mutate: add or modify columns
  • summarise: reduce a bunch of rows down to a single summary row.
  • group_by: group the data so that the above operations apply per group.

We start by reading in some data on the number of school students in New Zealand broken down by school, ethnicity and level. The data comes from

https://www.educationcounts.govt.nz/statistics/school-rolls

and is read in using:

roll <- read_csv("http://www.massey.ac.nz/~jcmarsha/data/schools/roll.csv")
## Rows: 66261 Columns: 4
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## Delimiter: ","
## chr (3): School, EthnicGroup, Level
## dbl (1): Students
## 
## ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
## ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
roll
## # A tibble: 66,261 × 4
##    School                  EthnicGroup Level   Students
##    <chr>                   <chr>       <chr>      <dbl>
##  1 3H School International Asian       Year 10        1
##  2 3H School International Asian       Year 7         2
##  3 3H School International European    Year 7         1
##  4 3H School International Pacific     Year 9         1
##  5 3H School International Asian       Year 9         1
##  6 A1 Student Limited      European    Year 10        1
##  7 A1 Student Limited      European    Year 11        1
##  8 A1 Student Limited      European    Year 4         2
##  9 A1 Student Limited      Asian       Year 5         1
## 10 A1 Student Limited      European    Year 5         2
## # ℹ 66,251 more rows

You should see there are 66,261 rows for 4 different variables (School, EthnicGroup, Level, Students)

We’ll start by using filter to select rows that satisfy one or more conditions. This is useful for quickly obtaining subsets of interest (indeed, the subset command from base R works similarly).

This next code block filters all rows where EthnicGroup is set to Māori. Notice the use of two equals for ‘is equal to’

filter(roll, EthnicGroup == "Māori")
## # A tibble: 16,798 × 4
##    School              EthnicGroup Level   Students
##    <chr>               <chr>       <chr>      <dbl>
##  1 ACG Parnell College Māori       Year 11        1
##  2 ACG Parnell College Māori       Year 13        1
##  3 ACG Parnell College Māori       Year 7         2
##  4 ACG Parnell College Māori       Year 8         1
##  5 ACG Strathallan     Māori       Year 10        9
##  6 ACG Strathallan     Māori       Year 11        5
##  7 ACG Strathallan     Māori       Year 12        5
##  8 ACG Strathallan     Māori       Year 13        2
##  9 ACG Strathallan     Māori       Year 6         1
## 10 ACG Strathallan     Māori       Year 7         4
## # ℹ 16,788 more rows
filter(roll, Level == "Year 7")
## # A tibble: 7,086 × 4
##    School                  EthnicGroup              Level  Students
##    <chr>                   <chr>                    <chr>     <dbl>
##  1 3H School International Asian                    Year 7        2
##  2 3H School International European                 Year 7        1
##  3 A1 Student Limited      European                 Year 7        5
##  4 ACG Parnell College     Māori                    Year 7        2
##  5 ACG Parnell College     Asian                    Year 7      152
##  6 ACG Parnell College     MELAA                    Year 7        1
##  7 ACG Parnell College     Other                    Year 7        2
##  8 ACG Parnell College     European                 Year 7       16
##  9 ACG Parnell College     International fee paying Year 7        2
## 10 ACG Strathallan         Māori                    Year 7        4
## # ℹ 7,076 more rows

A note about macrons

The above line of code for filtering our roll data by EthnicGroup utilises a macron in the word “Māori”. On many devices this can be a bit tricky to type in! In most systems (Mac, Windows, Linux) you can do this by installing the Māori keyboard setup. Another way is to use copy and paste. e.g. if we do something like:

count(roll, EthnicGroup)
## # A tibble: 7 × 2
##   EthnicGroup                  n
##   <chr>                    <int>
## 1 Asian                    11448
## 2 European                 15696
## 3 International fee paying  1520
## 4 MELAA                     7623
## 5 Māori                    16798
## 6 Other                     3291
## 7 Pacific                   9885

this shows us how many rows there are of each entry in EthnicGroup. We can then copy and paste “Māori” entry into our code. As macrons are super important in te reo and many other languages, we will try and use them wherever we can.

More than one row may be selected by separating different conditions with commas (for AND) or the vertical pipe symbol (for OR). e.g. compare the following:

filter(roll, EthnicGroup == "Māori", Level == "Year 1")
## # A tibble: 1,795 × 4
##    School                      EthnicGroup Level  Students
##    <chr>                       <chr>       <chr>     <dbl>
##  1 Abbotsford School           Māori       Year 1        6
##  2 Aberdeen School             Māori       Year 1       32
##  3 Addington Te Kura Taumatua  Māori       Year 1        9
##  4 Adventure School            Māori       Year 1        3
##  5 Ahipara School              Māori       Year 1       24
##  6 Ahuroa School               Māori       Year 1        4
##  7 Ahutoetoe School            Māori       Year 1        1
##  8 Aidanfield Christian School Māori       Year 1        3
##  9 Aka Aka School              Māori       Year 1        6
## 10 Akaroa Area School          Māori       Year 1        1
## # ℹ 1,785 more rows
filter(roll, EthnicGroup == "Māori" | Level == "Year 1")
## # A tibble: 21,976 × 4
##    School              EthnicGroup Level   Students
##    <chr>               <chr>       <chr>      <dbl>
##  1 ACG Parnell College Asian       Year 1        27
##  2 ACG Parnell College European    Year 1         2
##  3 ACG Parnell College Māori       Year 11        1
##  4 ACG Parnell College Māori       Year 13        1
##  5 ACG Parnell College Māori       Year 7         2
##  6 ACG Parnell College Māori       Year 8         1
##  7 ACG Strathallan     Pacific     Year 1         3
##  8 ACG Strathallan     Asian       Year 1        47
##  9 ACG Strathallan     Other       Year 1         1
## 10 ACG Strathallan     European    Year 1         4
## # ℹ 21,966 more rows

A helper function for ‘one of these options’ is %in%. e.g. these two are equivalent:

filter(roll, Level == "Year 1" | Level == "Year 2" | Level == "Year 3" | Level == "Year 4")
## # A tibble: 27,989 × 4
##    School              EthnicGroup Level  Students
##    <chr>               <chr>       <chr>     <dbl>
##  1 A1 Student Limited  European    Year 4        2
##  2 ACG Parnell College Asian       Year 1       27
##  3 ACG Parnell College European    Year 1        2
##  4 ACG Parnell College Asian       Year 2       48
##  5 ACG Parnell College MELAA       Year 2        1
##  6 ACG Parnell College European    Year 2        3
##  7 ACG Parnell College Pacific     Year 3        1
##  8 ACG Parnell College Asian       Year 3       35
##  9 ACG Parnell College Other       Year 3        1
## 10 ACG Parnell College European    Year 3        5
## # ℹ 27,979 more rows
filter(roll, Level %in% c("Year 1", "Year 2", "Year 3", "Year 4"))
## # A tibble: 27,989 × 4
##    School              EthnicGroup Level  Students
##    <chr>               <chr>       <chr>     <dbl>
##  1 A1 Student Limited  European    Year 4        2
##  2 ACG Parnell College Asian       Year 1       27
##  3 ACG Parnell College European    Year 1        2
##  4 ACG Parnell College Asian       Year 2       48
##  5 ACG Parnell College MELAA       Year 2        1
##  6 ACG Parnell College European    Year 2        3
##  7 ACG Parnell College Pacific     Year 3        1
##  8 ACG Parnell College Asian       Year 3       35
##  9 ACG Parnell College Other       Year 3        1
## 10 ACG Parnell College European    Year 3        5
## # ℹ 27,979 more rows
#1
filter(roll, School == "Queen Elizabeth College", Level == "Year 9")
## # A tibble: 3 × 4
##   School                  EthnicGroup Level  Students
##   <chr>                   <chr>       <chr>     <dbl>
## 1 Queen Elizabeth College Māori       Year 9       40
## 2 Queen Elizabeth College Pacific     Year 9        7
## 3 Queen Elizabeth College European    Year 9       14
#2
filter(roll, Level == "Year 9", Students > 100)
## # A tibble: 188 × 4
##    School                         EthnicGroup Level  Students
##    <chr>                          <chr>       <chr>     <dbl>
##  1 ACG Parnell College            Asian       Year 9      158
##  2 Albany Junior High School      European    Year 9      127
##  3 Aorere College                 Pacific     Year 9      160
##  4 Aorere College                 Asian       Year 9      104
##  5 Aotea College                  European    Year 9      156
##  6 Ashburton College              European    Year 9      163
##  7 Auckland Girls' Grammar School Pacific     Year 9      133
##  8 Auckland Grammar School        Asian       Year 9      237
##  9 Auckland Grammar School        European    Year 9      142
## 10 Avondale College               Pacific     Year 9      139
## # ℹ 178 more rows
#3
filter(roll, str_starts(School, "G"))
## # A tibble: 2,102 × 4
##    School         EthnicGroup Level  Students
##    <chr>          <chr>       <chr>     <dbl>
##  1 Galatea School Māori       Year 1       13
##  2 Galatea School Pacific     Year 1        1
##  3 Galatea School European    Year 1        4
##  4 Galatea School Māori       Year 2       12
##  5 Galatea School Asian       Year 2        1
##  6 Galatea School Other       Year 2        1
##  7 Galatea School European    Year 2        9
##  8 Galatea School Māori       Year 3        8
##  9 Galatea School Asian       Year 3        1
## 10 Galatea School European    Year 3        7
## # ℹ 2,092 more rows

Try yourself:

  1. Find all rows corresponding to Queen Elizabeth College students in Year 9.
  2. Find all rows where there are more than 100 Year 9 Asian students.
  3. Find all rows where the school name starts with the letter ‘G’ (hint: you can use School > ‘G’ for this, but using str_starts might be better!)

You can arrange (sort) row output based on the values in each column, and can arrange by more than one column at once. The first column specified will be sorted first, and then all entries that are the same on that column will then be sorted by the second and multiple columns.

You can use desc() to sort in descending order.

arrange(roll, EthnicGroup, Level)
## # A tibble: 66,261 × 4
##    School                     EthnicGroup Level  Students
##    <chr>                      <chr>       <chr>     <dbl>
##  1 ACG Parnell College        Asian       Year 1       27
##  2 ACG Strathallan            Asian       Year 1       47
##  3 ACG Sunderland             Asian       Year 1       31
##  4 ACG Tauranga               Asian       Year 1        3
##  5 AGE School                 Asian       Year 1        1
##  6 Abbotsford School          Asian       Year 1        1
##  7 Aberdeen School            Asian       Year 1       17
##  8 Addington Te Kura Taumatua Asian       Year 1       21
##  9 Adventure School           Asian       Year 1       12
## 10 Ahuroa School              Asian       Year 1        1
## # ℹ 66,251 more rows
arrange(roll, desc(Students))
## # A tibble: 66,261 × 4
##    School                                 EthnicGroup Level   Students
##    <chr>                                  <chr>       <chr>      <dbl>
##  1 Te Aho o Te Kura Pounamu               European    Year 12      800
##  2 Te Puna Wai o Waipapa - Hagley College European    Year 13      782
##  3 Te Aho o Te Kura Pounamu               European    Year 15      758
##  4 Te Aho o Te Kura Pounamu               European    Year 13      733
##  5 Te Aho o Te Kura Pounamu               European    Year 11      568
##  6 Te Aho o Te Kura Pounamu               European    Year 14      553
##  7 Te Aho o Te Kura Pounamu               Māori       Year 15      468
##  8 Te Aho o Te Kura Pounamu               Māori       Year 11      449
##  9 Te Aho o Te Kura Pounamu               Māori       Year 12      396
## 10 Macleans College                       Asian       Year 10      372
## # ℹ 66,251 more rows
#1
arrange(roll, desc(Students))
## # A tibble: 66,261 × 4
##    School                                 EthnicGroup Level   Students
##    <chr>                                  <chr>       <chr>      <dbl>
##  1 Te Aho o Te Kura Pounamu               European    Year 12      800
##  2 Te Puna Wai o Waipapa - Hagley College European    Year 13      782
##  3 Te Aho o Te Kura Pounamu               European    Year 15      758
##  4 Te Aho o Te Kura Pounamu               European    Year 13      733
##  5 Te Aho o Te Kura Pounamu               European    Year 11      568
##  6 Te Aho o Te Kura Pounamu               European    Year 14      553
##  7 Te Aho o Te Kura Pounamu               Māori       Year 15      468
##  8 Te Aho o Te Kura Pounamu               Māori       Year 11      449
##  9 Te Aho o Te Kura Pounamu               Māori       Year 12      396
## 10 Macleans College                       Asian       Year 10      372
## # ℹ 66,251 more rows
#2
roll |>
  filter(EthnicGroup == "Pacific") |>
  arrange(desc(Students))
## # A tibble: 9,885 × 4
##    School                  EthnicGroup Level   Students
##    <chr>                   <chr>       <chr>      <dbl>
##  1 Kedgley Intermediate    Pacific     Year 7       289
##  2 Manurewa High School    Pacific     Year 9       259
##  3 Manurewa High School    Pacific     Year 11      245
##  4 Kedgley Intermediate    Pacific     Year 8       237
##  5 Manurewa Intermediate   Pacific     Year 8       226
##  6 Manurewa Intermediate   Pacific     Year 7       225
##  7 Manurewa High School    Pacific     Year 10      219
##  8 Papatoetoe Intermediate Pacific     Year 8       192
##  9 Manurewa High School    Pacific     Year 12      190
## 10 Papatoetoe Intermediate Pacific     Year 7       174
## # ℹ 9,875 more rows
#3
roll |> 
  filter(EthnicGroup == "International fee paying") |>
  arrange(desc(Students))
## # A tibble: 1,520 × 4
##    School                               EthnicGroup              Level  Students
##    <chr>                                <chr>                    <chr>     <dbl>
##  1 UP International College New Zealand International fee paying Year …       71
##  2 Macleans College                     International fee paying Year …       51
##  3 Rangitoto College                    International fee paying Year …       42
##  4 Rangitoto College                    International fee paying Year …       41
##  5 Avondale College                     International fee paying Year …       39
##  6 Macleans College                     International fee paying Year …       36
##  7 Waimea College                       International fee paying Year …       35
##  8 Takapuna Grammar School              International fee paying Year …       32
##  9 Burnside High School                 International fee paying Year …       31
## 10 Rangitoto College                    International fee paying Year …       31
## # ℹ 1,510 more rows

Try yourself

  1. Arrange the roll rows by number of students.
  2. Find all rows where the ethnicity is Pacific, arranging them by decreasing number of students. (Hint: First filter down to Pacific students, then arrange)
  3. Which school has the highest number of International fee paying students at Year 13? (Hint: ‘International fee paying’ is an option for EthnicGroup) == UP International College New Zealand

The pipe operator

Recall that the pipe operator is useful for combining these type of operations together.

In the last set of exercises you combined filter and arrange together. It is very common when data wrangling to have to combine multiple functions like this to get a ‘pipeline’ that goes from the original data frame to the subset, arranged how you like.

For example, your answer for finding which school has the highest number of international fee paying students at year 13 might look like

internationals = filter(roll, EthnicGroup == "International fee paying")
y13internationals = filter(internationals, Level == "Year 13")
arrange(y13internationals, desc(Students))
## # A tibble: 180 × 4
##    School                               EthnicGroup              Level  Students
##    <chr>                                <chr>                    <chr>     <dbl>
##  1 UP International College New Zealand International fee paying Year …       71
##  2 Rangitoto College                    International fee paying Year …       28
##  3 ACG Parnell College                  International fee paying Year …       22
##  4 Avondale College                     International fee paying Year …       22
##  5 Pakuranga College                    International fee paying Year …       21
##  6 Macleans College                     International fee paying Year …       20
##  7 Kerikeri High School                 International fee paying Year …       19
##  8 Howick College                       International fee paying Year …       17
##  9 Takapuna Grammar School              International fee paying Year …       16
## 10 Auckland Grammar School              International fee paying Year …       15
## # ℹ 170 more rows

The data frames internationals and y13internationals here are really only temporary - we only use them to make our code look a little more readable. We could instead do:

arrange(filter(filter(roll, EthnicGroup == "International fee paying"), Level == "Year 13"), desc(Students))
## # A tibble: 180 × 4
##    School                               EthnicGroup              Level  Students
##    <chr>                                <chr>                    <chr>     <dbl>
##  1 UP International College New Zealand International fee paying Year …       71
##  2 Rangitoto College                    International fee paying Year …       28
##  3 ACG Parnell College                  International fee paying Year …       22
##  4 Avondale College                     International fee paying Year …       22
##  5 Pakuranga College                    International fee paying Year …       21
##  6 Macleans College                     International fee paying Year …       20
##  7 Kerikeri High School                 International fee paying Year …       19
##  8 Howick College                       International fee paying Year …       17
##  9 Takapuna Grammar School              International fee paying Year …       16
## 10 Auckland Grammar School              International fee paying Year …       15
## # ℹ 170 more rows

But this is hard to read! Instead, we could use the pipe operator, |> (insertable via Ctrl-Shift-M, or Cmd-Shift-M on a Mac).

NOTE: If you press that key combination and instead get %>%, then go to Tools->Global Options->Code and select “use native pipe operator, |>”.

What this does is takes what you provide on the left hand side and “pipes” it as the first argument into the function you provide on the right hand side. Any other arguments to the function then just get placed in the function as usual. So the above could be written:

 arrange(filter(filter(roll, EthnicGroup == "International fee paying"), Level == "Year 13"),desc(Students))
## # A tibble: 180 × 4
##    School                               EthnicGroup              Level  Students
##    <chr>                                <chr>                    <chr>     <dbl>
##  1 UP International College New Zealand International fee paying Year …       71
##  2 Rangitoto College                    International fee paying Year …       28
##  3 ACG Parnell College                  International fee paying Year …       22
##  4 Avondale College                     International fee paying Year …       22
##  5 Pakuranga College                    International fee paying Year …       21
##  6 Macleans College                     International fee paying Year …       20
##  7 Kerikeri High School                 International fee paying Year …       19
##  8 Howick College                       International fee paying Year …       17
##  9 Takapuna Grammar School              International fee paying Year …       16
## 10 Auckland Grammar School              International fee paying Year …       15
## # ℹ 170 more rows

And be read “Take roll, filter so that EthnicGroup is International fee paying, then filter to Year 13 students and arrange by descending number of students.”

Try yourself

roll |>
  filter(EthnicGroup == "International fee paying") |>
  filter(Level == "Year 13") |>
  arrange(desc(Students))
## # A tibble: 180 × 4
##    School                               EthnicGroup              Level  Students
##    <chr>                                <chr>                    <chr>     <dbl>
##  1 UP International College New Zealand International fee paying Year …       71
##  2 Rangitoto College                    International fee paying Year …       28
##  3 ACG Parnell College                  International fee paying Year …       22
##  4 Avondale College                     International fee paying Year …       22
##  5 Pakuranga College                    International fee paying Year …       21
##  6 Macleans College                     International fee paying Year …       20
##  7 Kerikeri High School                 International fee paying Year …       19
##  8 Howick College                       International fee paying Year …       17
##  9 Takapuna Grammar School              International fee paying Year …       16
## 10 Auckland Grammar School              International fee paying Year …       15
## # ℹ 170 more rows
  1. Try doing a few of the above exercises utilising the pipe instead. i.e. all statements should start roll |>.
roll |>
  filter(Level == "Year 9")
## # A tibble: 2,393 × 4
##    School                  EthnicGroup              Level  Students
##    <chr>                   <chr>                    <chr>     <dbl>
##  1 3H School International Pacific                  Year 9        1
##  2 3H School International Asian                    Year 9        1
##  3 A1 Student Limited      European                 Year 9        3
##  4 ACG Parnell College     Pacific                  Year 9        1
##  5 ACG Parnell College     Asian                    Year 9      158
##  6 ACG Parnell College     MELAA                    Year 9        4
##  7 ACG Parnell College     Other                    Year 9        1
##  8 ACG Parnell College     European                 Year 9       24
##  9 ACG Parnell College     International fee paying Year 9       11
## 10 ACG Strathallan         Māori                    Year 9        8
## # ℹ 2,383 more rows
roll |>
  arrange(desc(Students))
## # A tibble: 66,261 × 4
##    School                                 EthnicGroup Level   Students
##    <chr>                                  <chr>       <chr>      <dbl>
##  1 Te Aho o Te Kura Pounamu               European    Year 12      800
##  2 Te Puna Wai o Waipapa - Hagley College European    Year 13      782
##  3 Te Aho o Te Kura Pounamu               European    Year 15      758
##  4 Te Aho o Te Kura Pounamu               European    Year 13      733
##  5 Te Aho o Te Kura Pounamu               European    Year 11      568
##  6 Te Aho o Te Kura Pounamu               European    Year 14      553
##  7 Te Aho o Te Kura Pounamu               Māori       Year 15      468
##  8 Te Aho o Te Kura Pounamu               Māori       Year 11      449
##  9 Te Aho o Te Kura Pounamu               Māori       Year 12      396
## 10 Macleans College                       Asian       Year 10      372
## # ℹ 66,251 more rows

The select command from dplyr allows you to select columns you wish to keep based on their name.

  • Ranges can be used using A:C to pick columns A through C
  • Columns can be removed through negation -A.
  • everything() will return all remaining columns.
  • starts_with() can be handy if you have several columns with a common prefix.

Try the following examples to understand what they do:

roll |> select(School, EthnicGroup, Students)
## # A tibble: 66,261 × 3
##    School                  EthnicGroup Students
##    <chr>                   <chr>          <dbl>
##  1 3H School International Asian              1
##  2 3H School International Asian              2
##  3 3H School International European           1
##  4 3H School International Pacific            1
##  5 3H School International Asian              1
##  6 A1 Student Limited      European           1
##  7 A1 Student Limited      European           1
##  8 A1 Student Limited      European           2
##  9 A1 Student Limited      Asian              1
## 10 A1 Student Limited      European           2
## # ℹ 66,251 more rows
roll |> select(-School, Number=Students)
## # A tibble: 66,261 × 3
##    EthnicGroup Level   Number
##    <chr>       <chr>    <dbl>
##  1 Asian       Year 10      1
##  2 Asian       Year 7       2
##  3 European    Year 7       1
##  4 Pacific     Year 9       1
##  5 Asian       Year 9       1
##  6 European    Year 10      1
##  7 European    Year 11      1
##  8 European    Year 4       2
##  9 Asian       Year 5       1
## 10 European    Year 5       2
## # ℹ 66,251 more rows
roll |> select(Students, Level, everything())
## # A tibble: 66,261 × 4
##    Students Level   School                  EthnicGroup
##       <dbl> <chr>   <chr>                   <chr>      
##  1        1 Year 10 3H School International Asian      
##  2        2 Year 7  3H School International Asian      
##  3        1 Year 7  3H School International European   
##  4        1 Year 9  3H School International Pacific    
##  5        1 Year 9  3H School International Asian      
##  6        1 Year 10 A1 Student Limited      European   
##  7        1 Year 11 A1 Student Limited      European   
##  8        2 Year 4  A1 Student Limited      European   
##  9        1 Year 5  A1 Student Limited      Asian      
## 10        2 Year 5  A1 Student Limited      European   
## # ℹ 66,251 more rows

Try yourself

  1. Rearrange the roll data set so that Students and Level are first.
  2. Rename the School column to Name keeping all other columns.
  3. Try part 2 using rename instead of select.
#1
roll |> select(Students, Level, everything())
## # A tibble: 66,261 × 4
##    Students Level   School                  EthnicGroup
##       <dbl> <chr>   <chr>                   <chr>      
##  1        1 Year 10 3H School International Asian      
##  2        2 Year 7  3H School International Asian      
##  3        1 Year 7  3H School International European   
##  4        1 Year 9  3H School International Pacific    
##  5        1 Year 9  3H School International Asian      
##  6        1 Year 10 A1 Student Limited      European   
##  7        1 Year 11 A1 Student Limited      European   
##  8        2 Year 4  A1 Student Limited      European   
##  9        1 Year 5  A1 Student Limited      Asian      
## 10        2 Year 5  A1 Student Limited      European   
## # ℹ 66,251 more rows
#2
roll |> select(Name = School, everything())
## # A tibble: 66,261 × 4
##    Name                    EthnicGroup Level   Students
##    <chr>                   <chr>       <chr>      <dbl>
##  1 3H School International Asian       Year 10        1
##  2 3H School International Asian       Year 7         2
##  3 3H School International European    Year 7         1
##  4 3H School International Pacific     Year 9         1
##  5 3H School International Asian       Year 9         1
##  6 A1 Student Limited      European    Year 10        1
##  7 A1 Student Limited      European    Year 11        1
##  8 A1 Student Limited      European    Year 4         2
##  9 A1 Student Limited      Asian       Year 5         1
## 10 A1 Student Limited      European    Year 5         2
## # ℹ 66,251 more rows
#3
roll |> rename(Name = School, Ethnicity = EthnicGroup) |> select(Name, everything())
## # A tibble: 66,261 × 4
##    Name                    Ethnicity Level   Students
##    <chr>                   <chr>     <chr>      <dbl>
##  1 3H School International Asian     Year 10        1
##  2 3H School International Asian     Year 7         2
##  3 3H School International European  Year 7         1
##  4 3H School International Pacific   Year 9         1
##  5 3H School International Asian     Year 9         1
##  6 A1 Student Limited      European  Year 10        1
##  7 A1 Student Limited      European  Year 11        1
##  8 A1 Student Limited      European  Year 4         2
##  9 A1 Student Limited      Asian     Year 5         1
## 10 A1 Student Limited      European  Year 5         2
## # ℹ 66,251 more rows

mutate() adds new variables using functions of existing columns. You can overwrite columns as well.

roll |> mutate(Proportion = Students/sum(Students))
## # A tibble: 66,261 × 5
##    School                  EthnicGroup Level   Students Proportion
##    <chr>                   <chr>       <chr>      <dbl>      <dbl>
##  1 3H School International Asian       Year 10        1 0.00000120
##  2 3H School International Asian       Year 7         2 0.00000241
##  3 3H School International European    Year 7         1 0.00000120
##  4 3H School International Pacific     Year 9         1 0.00000120
##  5 3H School International Asian       Year 9         1 0.00000120
##  6 A1 Student Limited      European    Year 10        1 0.00000120
##  7 A1 Student Limited      European    Year 11        1 0.00000120
##  8 A1 Student Limited      European    Year 4         2 0.00000241
##  9 A1 Student Limited      Asian       Year 5         1 0.00000120
## 10 A1 Student Limited      European    Year 5         2 0.00000241
## # ℹ 66,251 more rows
clean = roll |> mutate(Level = as.numeric(substring(Level, 6)))

Try yourself

  1. Using the clean dataset, create a new column combining ethnicgroup and level with paste.
  2. Redo part 1, so that the original two columns are removed.
  3. Create a new variable “Age” with the typical age of students in each year level (Year 1 students are typically 5 years old).
  4. Investigate using the unite function from tidyr to do what you did in part 1 and 2.

Header

#1
roll |> mutate(Proportion = Students/sum(Students))
## # A tibble: 66,261 × 5
##    School                  EthnicGroup Level   Students Proportion
##    <chr>                   <chr>       <chr>      <dbl>      <dbl>
##  1 3H School International Asian       Year 10        1 0.00000120
##  2 3H School International Asian       Year 7         2 0.00000241
##  3 3H School International European    Year 7         1 0.00000120
##  4 3H School International Pacific     Year 9         1 0.00000120
##  5 3H School International Asian       Year 9         1 0.00000120
##  6 A1 Student Limited      European    Year 10        1 0.00000120
##  7 A1 Student Limited      European    Year 11        1 0.00000120
##  8 A1 Student Limited      European    Year 4         2 0.00000241
##  9 A1 Student Limited      Asian       Year 5         1 0.00000120
## 10 A1 Student Limited      European    Year 5         2 0.00000241
## # ℹ 66,251 more rows
clean = roll |> mutate(Level = as.numeric(substring(Level, 6)))
clean |> mutate(EthLevel = paste(EthnicGroup, Level))
## # A tibble: 66,261 × 5
##    School                  EthnicGroup Level Students EthLevel   
##    <chr>                   <chr>       <dbl>    <dbl> <chr>      
##  1 3H School International Asian          10        1 Asian 10   
##  2 3H School International Asian           7        2 Asian 7    
##  3 3H School International European        7        1 European 7 
##  4 3H School International Pacific         9        1 Pacific 9  
##  5 3H School International Asian           9        1 Asian 9    
##  6 A1 Student Limited      European       10        1 European 10
##  7 A1 Student Limited      European       11        1 European 11
##  8 A1 Student Limited      European        4        2 European 4 
##  9 A1 Student Limited      Asian           5        1 Asian 5    
## 10 A1 Student Limited      European        5        2 European 5 
## # ℹ 66,251 more rows
#2
clean |> mutate(EthLevel = EthnicGroup, Level) |>
  select(EthLevel, Students, School)
## # A tibble: 66,261 × 3
##    EthLevel Students School                 
##    <chr>       <dbl> <chr>                  
##  1 Asian           1 3H School International
##  2 Asian           2 3H School International
##  3 European        1 3H School International
##  4 Pacific         1 3H School International
##  5 Asian           1 3H School International
##  6 European        1 A1 Student Limited     
##  7 European        1 A1 Student Limited     
##  8 European        2 A1 Student Limited     
##  9 Asian           1 A1 Student Limited     
## 10 European        2 A1 Student Limited     
## # ℹ 66,251 more rows
#3
clean <- clean |> 
  mutate(Age = Level + 4, EthLevel = EthnicGroup, Level) |>
  select(EthLevel, Age, School, Students)

#4
roll |> mutate(Proportion = Students/sum(Students))
## # A tibble: 66,261 × 5
##    School                  EthnicGroup Level   Students Proportion
##    <chr>                   <chr>       <chr>      <dbl>      <dbl>
##  1 3H School International Asian       Year 10        1 0.00000120
##  2 3H School International Asian       Year 7         2 0.00000241
##  3 3H School International European    Year 7         1 0.00000120
##  4 3H School International Pacific     Year 9         1 0.00000120
##  5 3H School International Asian       Year 9         1 0.00000120
##  6 A1 Student Limited      European    Year 10        1 0.00000120
##  7 A1 Student Limited      European    Year 11        1 0.00000120
##  8 A1 Student Limited      European    Year 4         2 0.00000241
##  9 A1 Student Limited      Asian       Year 5         1 0.00000120
## 10 A1 Student Limited      European    Year 5         2 0.00000241
## # ℹ 66,251 more rows
clean = roll |> mutate(Level = as.numeric(substring(Level, 6)))
clean |> unite(EthLevel, EthnicGroup, Level)
## # A tibble: 66,261 × 3
##    School                  EthLevel    Students
##    <chr>                   <chr>          <dbl>
##  1 3H School International Asian_10           1
##  2 3H School International Asian_7            2
##  3 3H School International European_7         1
##  4 3H School International Pacific_9          1
##  5 3H School International Asian_9            1
##  6 A1 Student Limited      European_10        1
##  7 A1 Student Limited      European_11        1
##  8 A1 Student Limited      European_4         2
##  9 A1 Student Limited      Asian_5            1
## 10 A1 Student Limited      European_5         2
## # ℹ 66,251 more rows

The summarise() function reduces our dataset down to just a single row containing some summary. Try the following examples to understand what they do. Notice in the second example that we can use variables we’ve computed (Total) in other summaries.

clean |> summarise(Rows = n())
## # A tibble: 1 × 1
##    Rows
##   <int>
## 1 66261
clean |> summarise(Total = sum(Students), Average = Total/n())
## # A tibble: 1 × 2
##    Total Average
##    <dbl>   <dbl>
## 1 831038    12.5

Try yourself

#1
roll |> 
  summarise(maxstudents = max(Students))
## # A tibble: 1 × 1
##   maxstudents
##         <dbl>
## 1         800
#2
roll |> 
  summarise(medianstudents = median(Students))
## # A tibble: 1 × 1
##   medianstudents
##            <dbl>
## 1              5
#3
roll |>
summarise(min = min(Level), max = max(Level))
## # A tibble: 1 × 2
##   min    max   
##   <chr>  <chr> 
## 1 Year 1 Year 9
#4
clean |> 
  filter(EthnicGroup == "Māori") |>
  filter(Level == 9) |>
  summarise(sum(Students))
## # A tibble: 1 × 1
##   `sum(Students)`
##             <dbl>
## 1           17879
  1. Find the largest (maximum) number of students in any row.
  2. Find the median number of students in a row.
  3. What is the lowest and highest year levels in the data?
  4. Find the number of Māori students in year 9 (hint: filter then summarise)

The true power of dplyr comes with the group_by() operation, which allows all of the rest of the operations we’ve learned about (plus more!) to be performed simultaneously on subgroups of the data.

The idea with group_by() is to collect rows together to be treated as a unit using one or more variables. All the other commands then operate per-group. For example, we can easily find the number of students per school by grouping by school and then adding up the number of students with summarise:

clean |> group_by(School) |> summarise(Total = sum(Students))
## # A tibble: 2,534 × 2
##    School                  Total
##    <chr>                   <dbl>
##  1 3H School International     6
##  2 A1 Student Limited         22
##  3 ACG Parnell College      1682
##  4 ACG Strathallan          1111
##  5 ACG Sunderland            729
##  6 ACG Tauranga              387
##  7 ADDI Enrichment Academy    24
##  8 AGE School                 92
##  9 Abbotsford School         296
## 10 Aberdeen School           680
## # ℹ 2,524 more rows

Try yourself

  1. How many students of each ethnicity are there?
  2. Which school has the most students in year 13?
  3. Produce a graph of the total number of students by year level. Hint: First get the data frame you want, and then use ggplot with geom_col.
#1
clean |> group_by(EthnicGroup)|> summarise(Total = sum(Students))
## # A tibble: 7 × 2
##   EthnicGroup               Total
##   <chr>                     <dbl>
## 1 Asian                    139908
## 2 European                 365471
## 3 International fee paying   6228
## 4 MELAA                     24264
## 5 Māori                    206123
## 6 Other                      6730
## 7 Pacific                   82314
#2
clean |> 
  filter( Level == 13) |> 
  group_by(School) |> 
  summarise(Total = sum(Students)) |>
  arrange(desc(Total))
## # A tibble: 553 × 2
##    School                                 Total
##    <chr>                                  <dbl>
##  1 Te Aho o Te Kura Pounamu                1248
##  2 Te Puna Wai o Waipapa - Hagley College  1214
##  3 Rangitoto College                        563
##  4 Mt Albert Grammar School                 515
##  5 Avondale College                         478
##  6 Macleans College                         466
##  7 Auckland Grammar School                  423
##  8 Burnside High School                     417
##  9 Epsom Girls Grammar School               407
## 10 Westlake Boys High School                392
## # ℹ 543 more rows
#3 
clean |>
  group_by(Level) |>
  summarise(Total = sum(Students)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = Level, y = Total)) +
  geom_col(fill = 'thistle3') +
  labs(title = "Total Students by Year Level")

Missing values

You probably noticed earlier that when plotting the penguins data, ggplot informed us that it had removed missing values. These are recorded as NA in R, and will need to be accounted for when summarising:

penguins |> summarise(mean_flipper_length = mean(flipper_length_mm))
## # A tibble: 1 × 1
##   mean_flipper_length
##                 <dbl>
## 1                  NA
penguins |> summarise(mean_flipper_length = mean(flipper_length_mm, na.rm=TRUE))
## # A tibble: 1 × 1
##   mean_flipper_length
##                 <dbl>
## 1                201.

Adding na.rm=TRUE to the mean function will fix this.

Another way that missing values can turn up is where they are implicitly missing. e.g. in the clean roll data, there are no NA entries at all, however there is missing data. e.g. if we tried to find the average number students of each level across schools, we might try3:

clean |> group_by(Level, School) |>
  summarise(Students = sum(Students)) |>
  group_by(Level) |>
  summarise(MeanStudents = mean(Students))
## `summarise()` has regrouped the output.
## ℹ Summaries were computed grouped by Level and School.
## ℹ Output is grouped by Level.
## ℹ Use `summarise(.groups = "drop_last")` to silence this message.
## ℹ Use `summarise(.by = c(Level, School))` for per-operation grouping
##   (`?dplyr::dplyr_by`) instead.
## # A tibble: 15 × 2
##    Level MeanStudents
##    <dbl>        <dbl>
##  1     1         30.2
##  2     2         30.8
##  3     3         31.8
##  4     4         31.5
##  5     5         32.0
##  6     6         32.5
##  7     7         35.5
##  8     8         45.2
##  9     9        120. 
## 10    10        117. 
## 11    11        118. 
## 12    12        103. 
## 13    13         81.7
## 14    14         14.3
## 15    15         36.1

However, this will be wrong, as there are no entries where the number of students in a category is 0, yet we know ofcourse that some schools will have no students of a particular level, such as primary schools who are unlikely to have year 13 students!

clean |> filter(Students == 0)
## # A tibble: 0 × 4
## # ℹ 4 variables: School <chr>, EthnicGroup <chr>, Level <dbl>, Students <dbl>

We can see this by totalling up students by school and level, and then taking a look at some schools:

clean |> group_by(Level, School) |>
  summarise(Students = sum(Students)) |>
  filter(str_detect(School, "Grammar")) |>
  arrange(School)
## `summarise()` has regrouped the output.
## ℹ Summaries were computed grouped by Level and School.
## ℹ Output is grouped by Level.
## ℹ Use `summarise(.groups = "drop_last")` to silence this message.
## ℹ Use `summarise(.by = c(Level, School))` for per-operation grouping
##   (`?dplyr::dplyr_by`) instead.
## # A tibble: 38 × 3
## # Groups:   Level [13]
##    Level School                         Students
##    <dbl> <chr>                             <dbl>
##  1     9 Auckland Girls' Grammar School      217
##  2    10 Auckland Girls' Grammar School      237
##  3    11 Auckland Girls' Grammar School      244
##  4    12 Auckland Girls' Grammar School      191
##  5    13 Auckland Girls' Grammar School      168
##  6     9 Auckland Grammar School             497
##  7    10 Auckland Grammar School             554
##  8    11 Auckland Grammar School             581
##  9    12 Auckland Grammar School             521
## 10    13 Auckland Grammar School             423
## # ℹ 28 more rows

One fix for this is using the complete() function from tidyr. This adds additional combinations to the data, though note that we will need to ungroup() things first, so that it can operate across the levels simultaneously4:

clean |> group_by(Level, School) |>
  summarise(Students = sum(Students)) |>
  ungroup() |>
  complete(Level, School) |>
  filter(str_detect(School, "Grammar")) |>
  arrange(School)
## `summarise()` has regrouped the output.
## ℹ Summaries were computed grouped by Level and School.
## ℹ Output is grouped by Level.
## ℹ Use `summarise(.groups = "drop_last")` to silence this message.
## ℹ Use `summarise(.by = c(Level, School))` for per-operation grouping
##   (`?dplyr::dplyr_by`) instead.
## # A tibble: 105 × 3
##    Level School                         Students
##    <dbl> <chr>                             <dbl>
##  1     1 Auckland Girls' Grammar School       NA
##  2     2 Auckland Girls' Grammar School       NA
##  3     3 Auckland Girls' Grammar School       NA
##  4     4 Auckland Girls' Grammar School       NA
##  5     5 Auckland Girls' Grammar School       NA
##  6     6 Auckland Girls' Grammar School       NA
##  7     7 Auckland Girls' Grammar School       NA
##  8     8 Auckland Girls' Grammar School       NA
##  9     9 Auckland Girls' Grammar School      217
## 10    10 Auckland Girls' Grammar School      237
## # ℹ 95 more rows

We can also fill in these missing values, because we know they should be 0.

Try yourself

  1. Add the necessary code above to fill in the NA values to be 0. You can do this using the fill parameter of complete, or alternatively the replace_na() function.
clean |>
  group_by(Level, School) |>
  summarise(Students = sum(Students), .groups = "drop") |>
  complete(Level, School, fill = list(Students = 0)) |>
  filter(str_detect(School, "Grammar")) |>
  arrange(School)
## # A tibble: 105 × 3
##    Level School                         Students
##    <dbl> <chr>                             <dbl>
##  1     1 Auckland Girls' Grammar School        0
##  2     2 Auckland Girls' Grammar School        0
##  3     3 Auckland Girls' Grammar School        0
##  4     4 Auckland Girls' Grammar School        0
##  5     5 Auckland Girls' Grammar School        0
##  6     6 Auckland Girls' Grammar School        0
##  7     7 Auckland Girls' Grammar School        0
##  8     8 Auckland Girls' Grammar School        0
##  9     9 Auckland Girls' Grammar School      217
## 10    10 Auckland Girls' Grammar School      237
## # ℹ 95 more rows
  1. With the NA values replaced, recompute the mean number of students by level across all schools.
clean |>
  group_by(Level, School) |>
  summarise(Students = sum(Students))|>
  ungroup()|>
  complete(School, Level, fill=list(Students = 0)) |>
  group_by(Level) |>
  summarise(meanstudents = mean(Students))
## `summarise()` has regrouped the output.
## ℹ Summaries were computed grouped by Level and School.
## ℹ Output is grouped by Level.
## ℹ Use `summarise(.groups = "drop_last")` to silence this message.
## ℹ Use `summarise(.by = c(Level, School))` for per-operation grouping
##   (`?dplyr::dplyr_by`) instead.
## # A tibble: 15 × 2
##    Level meanstudents
##    <dbl>        <dbl>
##  1     1       23.8  
##  2     2       24.3  
##  3     3       25.2  
##  4     4       24.9  
##  5     5       25.4  
##  6     6       25.7  
##  7     7       30.9  
##  8     8       26.8  
##  9     9       26.7  
## 10    10       26.4  
## 11    11       26.0  
## 12    12       22.5  
## 13    13       17.8  
## 14    14        0.559
## 15    15        0.883

  1. A common issue is loading MASS after dplyr - the MASS::select() function then overrides dplyr::select() which results in very opaque error messages when you try and select() columns from a data.frame.↩︎

  2. This is an example of the bias-variance trade-off. By smoothing more we will reduce the variance of the estimate (as we’re estimating the density at each location by using more of the data) but will increase bias (as the estimate depends on points far away from the location). Conversely, by smoothing less we decrease bias (the estimate depends only on points nearby) but increase variance (we’re using fewer points to estimate at each location). At some point we have a sweet spot! We’ll see this same principle over and over in this course.↩︎

  3. Note that the second group_by(Level) here is not required: After we summarise(), the second group (School) is automatically stripped, as we only have one row for that (as we’ve summarised across the different schools now). So the grouping will have already dropped down to Level. Note that summarise() will tell you this.↩︎

  4. You can also ungroup by adding the group="drop" to the summarise() command.↩︎
